publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 165, folios 64-65. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Dearest Mate. I have just been reading one of the most splendid sermons I ever had the blessing of perusing, by a preacher of some celebrity-- Mr Melville. He is one of the strictly orthodox, but I do not admire his sermon, (which has nothing to do with disputed points), the less
on that account. The subject is-- "On the Advantages of possessing the Scriptures"-- considered both individually & internally-- an advantage I feel myself to have daily & continually neglected.--

This subject reminds me that there is here a famous Unitarian preacher, whom the Hen thinks of going to hear next Sunday. Would you object to my going?-- Mama says that I may go separately if I please, but that in your absence she cannot be so far responsible for my proceeding
as to take me with her.--
By the bye I hope you will be here this week, but you make no mention of leaving Ashley.--

I called yesterday on Emily with Hester early in the day, & saw her. As we entered the room she pretended to be engrossed with the newspaper & to start up when we were almost close to her. She was very evidently & very much embarrassed the whole time, & I did not hasten my departure. I confounded her utterly

& made her convict herself, about her ride on the Henfield road, and had the pleasure of seeing her eyes quail & look into her lap beneath the very steady, though I believe very gentle, gaze of mine. I was determined to look her through for once. She coloured up to her temples & looked the picture of guilt. To be forced by me, without a single harsh word, to admit in Hester's presence that which she had previously denied to her, must have been unpleasant. I think she is probably
